California Stewardship Alliance
▾
Educate Lawmakers · Change the Law
01
Build an Enduring Movement
Local Stewardship Chapters in key cities — natural connectors leading door knockers, council liaisons, and letter writers, backed by professional messaging. The presence no opposing campaign can manufacture.
02
Support City Government
Give city leaders the tools and strategies they need to push back against one-size-fits-all policies.
03
Fundraising
Build the financial strength needed to protect California’s communities and connect supporters with high-impact efforts.
04
Align Capital
Redirect capital by realigning incentives — so the most profitable housing jobs are the ones that benefit the communities and citizens they’re built for.
05
Change the Constitution
Pass the Balanced Housing Growth Management Act — a California constitutional amendment requiring Sacramento to measure infrastructure capacity and fund growth before mandating it. Capacity before growth, written into law permanently.
California Stewardship Alliance3 / 21
The Crisis

There is a housing crisis —
but it's not the one they talk about

California Stewardship Alliance 4 / 21
The RHNA Mandate
Sacramento Built a Mandate on Data It No Longer Believes
RHNA is demanding 2.5 million units on projections two revisions out of date — they want to build houses for people who don’t exist.
2019
45.5M
RHNA Basis
DOF projects 45.5M Californians by 2060. HCD uses this figure to set 2.5M unit mandates through 2031.
▶
2021
44.0M
First Revision
Post-COVID reality begins to register. DOF revises projection down by 1.5M people. HCD mandates unchanged.
▶
2023
39.5M
Current Forecast
DOF projects essentially zero growth to 2060 — 6M below 2019. RHNA mandates still anchored to 2019 data.
The mandate has never been updated. HCD is requiring 2.5 million units by 2031 based on a population forecast the state’s own demographers abandoned. The 2023 projection shows California adding essentially zero net residents by 2060.

of RHNA goal

California Stewardship Alliance 6 / 21
The Problem
The Definition
of Insanity.
California has passed 275+ housing laws since 1982. The problem got worse every decade.
1982
Housing Accountability Act. "Prevent cities from blocking compliant housing." First of many.
2×
Median home prices doubled over the same period the laws were accumulating.
0
Change in homeownership rate over 30 years of mandates, amendments, and overhauls.
+132
More bills in the 2026 pipeline. Session not over. The cycle continues.
"The patches tell you where it breaks. Enough patches, and they tell you why."
California Stewardship Alliance7 / 21
The Outcome

This is not a policy anomaly.
It is the predicted endpoint.

California Stewardship Alliance 8 / 21
Economic Theory
What Economic Theory Actually Predicts
The results of the last decade are not a surprise to economic theory. They are its prediction.
Henry George
1879 — Progress and Poverty
When land supply is inelastic, economic growth transfers wealth from workers to landowners through rising rents. State-mandated upzoning without value capture is a precise realization of that mechanism.
Tiebout
Sorting Theory
Upzoning expensive jurisdictions accelerates displacement rather than integration. Mandated density overrides the market signal without addressing its cause — and exports the problem to adjacent communities.
Myrdal
Cumulative Causation
Without deliberate counterforce, markets diverge. Rich places attract investment, raising land values, pricing out workers, concentrating poverty at the periphery. The spiral does not self-correct. It accelerates.
California Stewardship Alliance 9 / 21
The CSA Strategy
Back to First Principles
Two Phases. One Strategy.
JOSH
Jurisdictional Objective Safety for Housing
Protect cities now. Build the coalition.
Legally defensible under existing state law — no constitutional change required
Gives cities objective, measurable capacity standards they can enforce today
Every city that adopts JOSH grows the coalition and proves the measurement works
Generates the data and political proof that BHF requires to win
⟷
BHF
Balanced Housing Framework
Reform the constitution. Make it permanent.
Constitutional standard for capacity-based growth — statewide and permanent
Converts the measurement JOSH proves into enforceable law
Removes Sacramento's ability to mandate density beyond infrastructure capacity
The coalition JOSH builds is the political force that passes it
We stopped asking how to fix the law. We asked what the law should measure. The answer became JOSH. The permanence became BHF.
California Stewardship Alliance 10 / 21
First Principles · The Ballot Measure
California Stewardship Alliance 11 / 21
Reality Informs.
First Principles Don't Lie.
01
Physical Capacity
02
Economic Reality
03
Human Livability
The fire marshal doesn't negotiate with politics. Neither does a road. Physical capacity is a fact, not a position — and every California housing failure traces back to the moment we stopped measuring it.
This doesn't have to be this hard. When the question is grounded in physical fact, the answer stops being political.
The Principle You already know this rule
California Stewardship Alliance · JOSH 12 / 21
🚪
The Building
A fire marshal sets maximum occupancy based on exit capacity.
That number is not a preference. It is not discretionary. It is the math of how many people can get through the door before they die.

Nobody argues with it. It is simply law.
🏔️
The Evacuation Corridor
A wildfire evacuation road is a door. It has a carrying capacity.
When fire comes, every resident behind that road needs to clear it within a defined time window.

Add more people than the road can carry — and the math stops working. People don't get out.
The same principle. The same math. A standard cities have never had for roads.
JOSH Jurisdictional Objective Standards for Housing
California Stewardship Alliance 13 / 21
The Tool
Cities can defend
themselves — with math.
In collaboration with Worcester Polytechnic Institute, we are bringing cities a technically validated, capacity-based measurement — deployable in weeks, not years, at a fraction of traditional study costs.
The objective standard JOSH establishes today becomes the foundation BHF makes permanent.
Balanced Housing Framework California Stewardship Alliance
California Stewardship Alliance 14 / 21
Balanced Housing Growth Management Act  ·  California Constitutional Amendment
Capacity Before Growth.
“
The State of California shall not impose housing mandates on any community without first establishing its physical capacity and funding the infrastructure required to support its growth.
Ballot Measure Language
California's housing crisis isn't a shortage of mandates. It's that Sacramento mandates growth without measuring what a community can absorb — or funding the infrastructure to support it.
Balanced Housing Framework I · Measure
California Stewardship Alliance 15 / 21
I
The First Step
Measure.
Before any mandate, every street, sewer, school, power line, and water system is measured against a credentialed national standard. The number is objective. A court can verify it in an afternoon.
Engineering facts become the foundation. Growth decisions stop being political.
Balanced Housing Framework II · Invest
California Stewardship Alliance 16 / 21
II
The Second Step
Invest.
Before any mandate, Sacramento funds the infrastructure required to support growth. Roads, water, sewer, transit, evacuation routes. Investment comes first. That's not a regulation — that's a commitment.
A state that invests before it asks earns the right to lead.
Balanced Housing Framework III · Grow
California Stewardship Alliance 17 / 21
III
The Third Step
Grow.
Any city ready to grow can apply for capacity certification. Approved cities receive infrastructure funding, tax incentives, and streamlined permitting — the tools needed to build what growth actually requires. Sacramento doesn't mandate growth. It funds the conditions that make it possible.
For the first time, Sacramento puts its money where its mandates are.
Balanced Housing Framework IV · Freedom & Equity
California Stewardship Alliance 18 / 21
IV
The Fourth Principle
Freedom & Equity.
No city is forced to grow. But the moment a city chooses to add homes, a minimum share must be within reach of the families who make a community work. Not aspirational. Constitutional.
The public creates the conditions for growth. The public shares in what growth produces.
California Stewardship Alliance Balanced Housing Framework
California Stewardship Alliance 19 / 21
Measured. Funded. Constitutional.
Housing that works for everyone isn't a wish —
it's ready.
Will you join us?

The Ask
What Your Investment Does
Each level funds a specific, measurable outcome. Your commitment tonight has a direct address.
i
Community Defender
$5,000
Funds neighborhood advocacy resources and legal strategy support for one community facing an immediate threat.
ii
Regional Steward
$10,000
Funds one full city government engagement — legal strategy, planning tools, and municipal support for one jurisdiction.
iii
Founding Ally
$25,000
Funds one full legislative district campaign — candidate research, voter outreach, and accountability tracking.
iv
Lead Steward
$50,000+
Funds Phase 2 statewide expansion — new district engagements, expanded research capacity, and the Balanced Housing Framework constitutional groundwork.
Giving levels reflected on the inside of your place card
